title: EEG Attention Detection
desc: ML pipeline classifying attention states from EEG brainwave data. Detects engagement vs low attention while viewing visual content. ~64% accuracy across unseen subjects.

detail.body:
Spring 2026 · Signals · Machine Learning

Full project and code: https://github.com/andersvestrum/eeg-attention-analysis

Project hosted on a collaborator's repository. We led development and analysis.

[SECTION: What this is]

This project analyzes EEG brainwave data to detect when someone is engaged or mentally checked out while looking at visual content.

[SECTION: Why we built this]

We were interested in what happens during attention and low engagement, especially in cases like doomscrolling where someone keeps watching but is not fully engaged.

The goal was to see if brain signals alone can tell the difference between active thinking and passive attention.

[SECTION: What we learned]

We learned that brain signals are noisy, but patterns still exist if the data is represented correctly.

- Feature choice matters more than model complexity
- Different brain states can be separated using frequency bands
- Real data requires careful preprocessing

[SECTION: How it works]

The pipeline starts with raw EEG recordings. The signals are split into short time windows and converted into features based on frequency bands.

Key bands:
- Theta (4-7 Hz)
- Alpha (8-12 Hz)
- Beta (13-30 Hz)

[IMAGE: assets/img/eeg-frequency-bands.png | Frequency band features extracted from EEG signals]

From these, features like alpha power, beta power, and alpha/beta ratio are computed.

These features represent different brain states:
- Higher alpha = more idle or low engagement
- Higher beta = more active thinking

Next, the data is used to train machine learning models. We compared Logistic Regression and XGBoost.

[IMAGE: assets/img/eeg-model-results.png | Model performance comparison]

The final model (XGBoost) reached about 64% accuracy on unseen subjects, showing that it learned meaningful patterns in the data.

[SECTION: Results]

- ~64% classification accuracy
- Alpha and beta bands were the most useful features
- Model generalized across different subjects

[SECTION: Conclusion]

This project helped us understand how signal processing and machine learning can work together to extract meaning from biological data.

These ideas can be used in brain-computer interfaces, attention monitoring systems, and adaptive user interfaces.
